name: Ochosi
description: Ochosi, also known as Oxossi, is a prominent Orisha in Yoruba religion and its diasporic traditions, including Santería and Candomblé. He is revered as the divine hunter, tracker, and scout. Ochosi is associated with justice, swift action, and precision. He is often depicted with a bow and arrow, symbolizing his ability to hit his target with accuracy and unwavering focus. Ochosi's origins are rooted in the ancient hunting societies of West Africa. He is believed to possess incredible knowledge of the forest, the animals within it, and the medicinal properties of plants. As a master of the hunt, he provides sustenance and protection to his followers. He is a skilled strategist and problem-solver, helping people navigate difficult situations and find their way through life's challenges. Ochosi works closely with Ogun, the Orisha of iron and technology, as they represent complementary forces: Ogun clears the path with his machete, while Ochosi finds the best route to follow. Ochosi's power lies not only in his hunting skills but also in his ability to bring justice and order. He is often invoked in legal matters and situations where fairness and truth are sought. His devotees often seek his guidance in finding lost items or uncovering hidden information. He is considered a protector of the innocent and a champion of those who have been wronged. Offerings to Ochosi typically include fruits, vegetables, and animals associated with the hunt.
subtitle: The Divine Hunter and Tracker

keyMyths.title: Ochosi and the Bird
keyMyths.description: Ochosi demonstrates his unparalleled skill by shooting a tiny bird from a great distance, proving his accuracy and mastery of the bow.

keyMyths.title: Ochosi and Ogun's Partnership
keyMyths.description: Ochosi and Ogun work together, Ogun clearing the path and Ochosi finding the best way to navigate it, showcasing their complementary skills.

keyMyths.title: Ochosi and Justice
keyMyths.description: A story tells of Ochosi using his hunting skills to uncover a crime and bring the guilty to justice, highlighting his role as a protector of the innocent.

sources.author: Baba Ifa Karade
